paraphrases: Always push over SSH; never embed a personal access token in the remote URL.
Use SSH keys for pushing code, and keep PATs out of repository URLs.
Avoid putting access tokens inside git remotes; authenticate with SSH instead.
Pushes should rely on SSH authentication rather than tokens baked into the URL.
Write to a temp file then rename so readers never observe a partial save.
Persist data atomically: stage into a scratch file and swap it into place.
Use the write-temp-then-rename pattern to avoid corrupting files mid-write.
To prevent half-written files, save to a temporary path and atomically rename.
Bind query values as parameters; never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
Stop building SQL by string interpolation — use prepared statements with placeholders.
Pass arguments through bound parameters to keep injection out of your queries.
Queries must use placeholders, not raw string concatenation of untrusted input.
Keep API keys in environment variables, never hardcoded in source files.
Credentials belong in a secrets vault or env, not committed to the repo.
Never check passwords into version control; load them from the environment.
Store sensitive tokens outside the codebase and inject them at runtime.
Retry failed network calls with exponential backoff and a little jitter.
On transient errors, wait progressively longer between attempts, adding randomness.
Use backoff that doubles each retry plus jitter to avoid thundering herds.
Failed remote requests should be retried with increasing, randomized delays.
Log routine events at info and reserve error for actual failures.
Pick the right severity: debug for tracing, error only when something breaks.
Don't spam error logs for normal flow — use info or debug appropriately.
Match log severity to the event so error truly means a fault occurred.
Gate unfinished functionality behind a toggle so it ships dark safely.
Wrap new behavior in a feature flag you can flip without redeploying.
Use runtime switches to enable incomplete features only when ready.
Ship risky changes disabled behind a flag and roll them out gradually.
Make schema changes additive first, then backfill, then drop old columns.
Roll out database migrations in expand-then-contract phases to stay backward compatible.
Add new columns before removing old ones so deploys don't break readers.
Migrate schemas incrementally: introduce, migrate data, and only later clean up.
Prefer immutable values; copy on change instead of mutating shared state.
Avoid in-place mutation — return new objects rather than editing existing ones.
Treat data structures as read-only and produce fresh copies for updates.
Don't mutate shared variables; favor immutability to prevent surprising aliasing bugs.
Store all timestamps in UTC and convert to local only for display.
Persist times as UTC; apply the user's timezone purely at the presentation layer.
Keep dates in UTC internally and localize them just when rendering.
Never save local time — record UTC and translate it when showing users.
Pin exact dependency versions in a lockfile for reproducible builds.
Commit the lockfile so everyone resolves identical package versions.
Lock down third-party versions to guarantee deterministic, repeatable installs.
Avoid floating version ranges — freeze dependencies through a checked-in lockfile.
Validate and sanitize incoming data at the boundary before trusting it.
Never trust client input; check and clean it as it enters the system.
Reject malformed requests early by validating fields at the edge.
Sanitize external input on arrival rather than deep inside business logic.
Paginate large list endpoints instead of returning every row at once.
Cap result sets with limit and offset so responses stay bounded.
Don't dump entire tables in one response — page through the results.
Break big collections into pages to keep payloads and memory reasonable.
Set sensible TTLs and bust cache entries the moment underlying data changes.
Invalidate cached values on write so stale reads never linger.
Give caches expiry times and clear them when the source updates.
Refresh or evict cache keys whenever the backing record is modified.
Make write operations idempotent so retries don't create duplicate effects.
Use idempotency keys so a repeated request applies the change only once.
Design mutations to be safely repeatable without double-charging or duplicating.
Guard endpoints with idempotency tokens to tolerate client retries cleanly.
Run the autoformatter before committing so style stays consistent everywhere.
Let a formatter enforce layout; don't bikeshed indentation in review.
Apply automatic formatting on save to keep diffs free of style noise.
Format code mechanically so reviewers focus on logic, not whitespace.
Keep unit tests independent — no shared state leaking between cases.
Each test should set up and tear down its own fixtures in isolation.
Avoid ordering dependencies; tests must pass regardless of run sequence.
Isolate test cases so one failure doesn't cascade into unrelated tests.
Throttle public APIs per client to protect the service from abuse.
Apply rate limits so a single caller can't overwhelm the backend.
Cap request frequency per key to keep traffic within safe bounds.
Limit how many calls each consumer makes within a time window.
Use multi-stage builds to ship slim images without build toolchains.
Keep container images small by discarding compilers in the final stage.
Trim image bloat with multi-stage Dockerfiles and minimal base layers.
Drop unnecessary build artifacts so the runtime image stays lightweight.
Handle SIGTERM to finish in-flight work before the process exits.
Drain active requests on shutdown rather than killing connections abruptly.
Catch termination signals and close resources cleanly before stopping.
Let the server complete pending tasks when asked to shut down.
Hash passwords with a slow algorithm like bcrypt, never store them plaintext.
Use a salted, adaptive hash for credentials instead of raw or MD5.
Protect stored passwords with bcrypt or argon2, not reversible encryption.
Never keep passwords readable — run them through a strong, salted hash.
Eager-load relations to avoid firing one query per row in a loop.
Batch related lookups instead of hitting the database inside iterations.
Prevent N+1 queries by joining or prefetching associated records upfront.
Don't query per item in a loop — fetch the associations in one shot.
Write commit subjects in the imperative mood under fifty characters.
Phrase commits as commands and keep the summary line short.
Use imperative, concise subject lines for every commit you make.
Commit titles should read like instructions and stay brief.
Make the pipeline fail fast so broken builds surface within minutes.
Order CI stages to catch the cheapest failures before slow ones run.
Stop the build early on the first error to shorten feedback loops.
Run quick checks first so developers learn of breakage quickly.
Configure environment-specific values through env vars, not hardcoded constants.
Externalize settings that differ per environment rather than baking them in.
Read deployment configuration from the environment to keep code portable.
Don't bake host or port into source; pull them from configuration.
Model absence with an option type instead of returning bare null.
Avoid null by wrapping maybe-missing values in an explicit optional.
Prefer Option or Result over null to make emptiness type-checked.
Express possibly-absent results with optionals, not silent null returns.
Load-test before launch to find the breaking point under realistic traffic.
Simulate peak concurrency early to discover where the system saturates.
Stress the service with synthetic load to size capacity before release.
Run performance benchmarks against expected volume prior to going live.
Trip a circuit breaker when a downstream dependency keeps failing.
Stop calling an unhealthy service temporarily to let it recover.
Use a breaker to short-circuit repeated failures instead of hammering it.
Open the circuit on sustained errors so failures don't cascade upstream.
Version public APIs so old clients keep working after you evolve them.
Introduce a new API version rather than breaking existing consumers.
Expose versioned endpoints to change contracts without disrupting callers.
Preserve compatibility by adding versions instead of altering responses in place.
Grant each service only the permissions it actually needs, nothing more.
Scope credentials narrowly so a compromise has limited blast radius.
Follow least privilege: minimal access rights for every account and role.
Restrict roles to the smallest set of permissions required for the job.
Open the README with a quickstart that gets someone running in minutes.
Lead documentation with copy-paste setup steps before deep explanation.
Put a fast install-and-run section at the top of the readme.
Front-load the README with the shortest path to a working example.
Unsubscribe listeners and clear timers when a component is destroyed.
Release event handlers on teardown to stop memory from leaking.
Always dispose of subscriptions so detached objects can be collected.
Clean up callbacks and intervals during unmount to avoid leaks.
Wrap errors with context as they propagate so failures are traceable.
Attach the operation that failed when bubbling an error upward.
Add descriptive context to errors instead of rethrowing them bare.
Enrich propagated failures with where and why for easier debugging.
Deploy to an idle environment and switch traffic over once it's healthy.
Run two production stacks and flip the router after verifying the new one.
Use blue-green releases so rollback is just pointing back at the old stack.
Stand up the new version alongside the old, then cut traffic across.
Name branches with a type prefix and a short descriptive slug.
Prefix feature branches consistently so their purpose is obvious.
Adopt a branch convention like feat/ or fix/ followed by a topic.
Use structured branch names so tooling and humans can parse intent.
Define a schema for payloads and validate messages against it.
Enforce a documented contract so producers and consumers stay aligned.
Validate serialized data against an agreed schema before processing.
Pin the shape of exchanged documents with an explicit, versioned schema.
Guard shared mutable state with a lock to prevent data races.
Synchronize access to concurrent resources so updates don't interleave badly.
Protect cross-thread variables behind a mutex against race conditions.
Serialize writes to shared memory using locks to keep state consistent.
Compress and resize images so pages load fast on slow connections.
Serve appropriately sized, optimized assets instead of huge originals.
Shrink picture file sizes to cut bandwidth and speed up rendering.
Optimize media before shipping it so users download fewer bytes.
Never silently swallow exceptions; at minimum log them with context.
Avoid empty catch blocks that hide failures from operators.
Don't suppress errors quietly — surface or record every caught exception.
Catching an error to ignore it is forbidden; always handle or report it.
Expose a liveness endpoint so orchestrators can detect a hung process.
Provide a health route that reports whether dependencies are reachable.
Add a probe endpoint for load balancers to verify the instance is up.
Surface readiness via a dedicated check so scheduling stays accurate.
Comments should explain why a choice was made, not restate the code.
Document intent and rationale; the syntax already says what it does.
Write comments about reasoning, since the how is visible in the code.
Capture the motivation behind tricky code rather than narrating each line.
Encrypt all traffic with TLS, including internal service-to-service calls.
Require HTTPS everywhere so data in transit is never sent in clear.
Terminate plaintext nowhere — use TLS for external and internal links alike.
Protect connections with certificates so eavesdroppers can't read payloads.
Replace magic numbers with named constants that explain their meaning.
Give unexplained literals descriptive names instead of scattering raw values.
Extract hardcoded numbers into well-named constants for readability.
Avoid bare numeric literals; bind them to clearly named symbols.
Apply backpressure so producers slow down when the queue fills up.
Bound message buffers to stop fast publishers from exhausting memory.
Signal upstream to throttle when consumers can't keep pace.
Cap in-flight work so a slow consumer doesn't drown the system.
Seed random generators in tests so results are reproducible.
Fix the RNG seed to make stochastic test outcomes deterministic.
Pin randomness with a known seed so flaky tests become stable.
Control random sources in tests by setting an explicit seed.
Ship sensible defaults so the app runs with zero configuration.
Provide working out-of-the-box settings that users can override later.
Make every config option optional with a reasonable fallback value.
Default configuration should let newcomers start without any setup.
Mark records deleted with a flag instead of physically removing rows.
Use soft deletes so data can be recovered and audited later.
Tombstone entries rather than hard-deleting them from the table.
Keep deleted rows around with a status field for recovery and history.
Defer loading heavy modules until they're actually needed at runtime.
Split bundles and load chunks on demand to speed first paint.
Lazily import expensive code so startup stays fast.
Load components only when required instead of all upfront.
Record who changed what and when for every sensitive operation.
Keep an immutable audit trail of privileged actions for compliance.
Log actor, action, and timestamp on security-relevant changes.
Track all critical mutations so they can be reviewed afterward.
Keep each function focused on one responsibility, not a dozen.
Split bloated routines so every function does a single thing well.
Favor small, single-purpose functions over sprawling multi-job ones.
A function should do one task; extract the rest into helpers.
Whitelist specific origins for cross-origin requests instead of wildcarding.
Lock CORS to trusted domains rather than allowing any site.
Restrict which origins may call your API; avoid a blanket allow-all.
Configure cross-origin policy narrowly to known frontends only.
Verify downloaded artifacts against a published checksum before using them.
Confirm file integrity with a hash to catch corruption or tampering.
Compare a computed digest to the expected one prior to installation.
Validate binaries by their checksum so you never run altered files.
Debounce rapid user input so handlers fire once after typing settles.
Coalesce bursts of events to avoid running work on every keystroke.
Wait for input to pause before triggering expensive operations.
Throttle keypress-driven actions so they execute only when typing stops.
Have a tested rollback path ready before shipping any release.
Never deploy without a rehearsed way to revert to the prior version.
Plan and verify how to undo a change before pushing it live.
Ensure every deployment can be reversed quickly if it misbehaves.
Use UUIDs for public identifiers so sequential IDs aren't guessable.
Prefer opaque random keys over incrementing integers in external URLs.
Expose non-enumerable identifiers to hide record counts and ordering.
Generate UUIDs for entities you reference in client-facing links.
Chase meaningful assertions, not a coverage percentage for its own sake.
Write tests that verify behavior rather than padding the coverage number.
High coverage means little without assertions that actually catch regressions.
Focus tests on real outcomes instead of gaming the coverage metric.
Stream big files in chunks rather than loading them fully into memory.
Process large uploads incrementally to keep memory usage flat.
Read and write huge data as a stream instead of buffering it all.
Handle oversized payloads piece by piece to avoid out-of-memory crashes.
Always quote shell variables to survive spaces and special characters.
Wrap variable expansions in double quotes to avoid word splitting.
Quote every interpolation in scripts so paths with spaces don't break.
Unquoted variables cause globbing bugs — quote them in shell code.
Scan dependencies for known vulnerabilities on every build.
Run a security audit of third-party packages regularly to catch CVEs.
Check installed libraries against advisory databases before shipping.
Automate vulnerability scanning of your supply chain in the pipeline.
Alert on symptoms users feel, not every internal metric blip.
Tune alarms to actionable conditions so on-call isn't drowned in noise.
Page humans only for real problems, not for benign threshold crossings.
Set alerts on user-facing impact rather than noisy low-level signals.
